articleSummary: The article, written in an informal tutorial/rant style for intermediate JavaScript developers, argues that JavaScript’s functional flexibility enables simpler, more elegant solutions compared to Java’s verbose, XML-heavy patterns. It contrasts imperative, recursive, and “bad” textbook Fibonacci implementations, praises Bluebird Promises over native ES6 or Angular’s $q, and highlights David Walsh’s 20-line debounce versus Java’s 500-line JDebounce library. The core thesis is that JavaScript’s first-class functions and concise idioms make it superior for tasks like async flows and rate limiting, while Java’s compile-time annotations and boilerplate unnecessarily complicate the same logic.
